channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEALSQ Deepens Partnership with Quantum Computing Startup EeroQ
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership highlights the growing convergence between quantum computing hardware development and the urgent need for quantum-resistant cybersecurity solutions. SEALSQ's investment in EeroQ's electron-on-helium quantum architecture is particularly noteworthy because this CMOS-compatible approach could potentially offer a more practical path to scalable quantum processors compared to traditional superconducting or trapped-ion systems. The collaboration makes strategic sense as SEALSQ's expertise in post-quantum cryptography pairs naturally with EeroQ's novel quantum computing platform, potentially creating an integrated solution that addresses both sides of the quantum revolution. While electron-on-helium qubits remain relatively early-stage compared to other quantum technologies, this partnership signals serious industry investment in alternative quantum computing approaches that could prove more manufacturable using existing semiconductor infrastructure.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ: LAES) (“SEALSQ” or the “Company”), a global leader in semiconductors, PKI, and post-quantum cybersecurity technologies, today announced a further strategic investment in EeroQ, the U.S.-based quantum chip design company pioneering a patented, breakthrough, and fully CMOS compatible quantum computing architecture based on electrons on helium (eHe). Building on the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: French National Quantum Update: May 2026
item.description:
Curator's Take: This substantial €1.55 billion commitment from France signals a major escalation in the global quantum race, positioning the country as a serious contender alongside established quantum powers like the US and China. The timing is particularly strategic as European nations increasingly recognize quantum computing as critical infrastructure for future economic competitiveness and national security. France's coordinated approach, combining significant capital investment with alignment to broader European industrial policy, could accelerate the development of a truly competitive European quantum ecosystem. This level of government backing provides the long-term funding stability that quantum hardware companies desperately need to bridge the gap between current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and fault-tolerant quantum computers.

Executive Brief France’s quantum sector is entering a more heavily capitalized and strategically coordinated phase, led by a major expansion of the country’s national investment agenda and deeper alignment with European industrial policy. In May 2026, French President Emmanuel Macron announced an additional €1 billion commitment to France’s national quantum plan alongside €550 million earmarked […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Who’s News: Strategic Leadership Appointments at Quandela, Zapata Quantum, Imperagen, and EigenQ
item.description:
Curator's Take: These strategic leadership appointments signal a critical maturation phase for the quantum computing industry, as companies transition from research-focused startups to commercially-viable enterprises requiring seasoned operational expertise. Quandela's addition of executives with deep tech scaling experience from established companies like Atos and Eutelsat reflects the growing confidence in photonic quantum computing's commercial potential and the need for proven leadership to navigate international expansion. The recruitment of talent from adjacent high-tech sectors like cybersecurity and aerospace demonstrates how quantum companies are leveraging expertise from industries that have successfully commercialized cutting-edge technologies. This wave of executive appointments across multiple quantum firms suggests the industry is entering a crucial phase where business execution and market strategy are becoming as important as technological breakthroughs.

Quandela has announced a major restructuring of its executive leadership and governance to drive the international scale-up of its photonic quantum computing hardware. Cyril Dujardin, an executive with 25 years of deep tech and cybersecurity experience at Atos and Eutelsat/OneWeb, has been named Chief Operating Officer (COO). Joining him is semiconductor and aerospace operations veteran [...] The post Who’s News: Strategic Leadership Appointments at Quandela, Zapata Quantum, Imperagen, and EigenQ appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Pramatra Space Secures Pre-Seed Capital to Advance Space-Based Quantum Key Distribution Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding round highlights the growing commercial viability of quantum key distribution (QKD) systems, particularly the challenging but promising space-based approach that could enable truly global quantum-secure communications. Pramatra's focus on satellite-based QKD hardware represents a significant technical leap beyond terrestrial fiber-optic systems, as space-based platforms can overcome the distance limitations that have historically constrained quantum cryptography networks. The backing from established space industry veterans like Pixxel's founder suggests increasing confidence that quantum security solutions are moving from laboratory curiosities to practical commercial applications. While terrestrial QKD networks are already operational in some regions, successful deployment of satellite-based systems could democratize access to quantum-secure communications globally, making this an important development to watch as quantum threats to classical encryption continue to evolve.

Pramatra Space Technology Pvt. Ltd., a deep-tech quantum security venture based in Bengaluru, has closed an undisclosed pre-seed financing round led by Seafund Ventures. The investment round included participation from Rebalance, Magnivia Ventures (co-investing with the Peaceful Progress Fund), and angel investors including Awais Ahmed, founder of earth observation company Pixxel Space. This financing supplements [...] The post Pramatra Space Secures Pre-Seed Capital to Advance Space-Based Quantum Key Distribution Hardware appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Stanford quantum computing breakthrough uses twisted light to work without extreme cooling
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Stanford breakthrough represents a potentially game-changing advance by demonstrating quantum entanglement at room temperature using twisted light beams, eliminating the need for the expensive dilution refrigerators that currently keep most quantum computers colder than outer space. The ability to create and manipulate entangled states without extreme cooling could dramatically reduce the cost and complexity of quantum systems, making them far more practical for widespread deployment. While photonic quantum computing has shown promise before, this approach of entangling photons with electrons using orbital angular momentum could open new pathways for hybrid quantum systems that combine the best of optical and electronic quantum technologies. If this technique scales effectively, it could accelerate the timeline for quantum computers to move from specialized research facilities into more accessible commercial applications.

A new room-temperature quantum device uses twisted light to entangle photons and electrons, overcoming one of the biggest hurdles in quantum technology. The breakthrough could pave the way for smaller, cheaper quantum systems with applications ranging from secure communications to future AI and computing platforms.

Read original at Science Daily - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Rigetti Simulates Plasma Wave Dispersion on Superconducting Ankaa-3 Processor Using Specialized Error Mitigation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Rigetti, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and the University of Colorado represents a significant step toward practical quantum simulations of complex physical phenomena, specifically demonstrating how quantum computers can model electromagnetic wave behavior in plasma environments. The use of specialized error mitigation techniques on Rigetti's Ankaa-3 superconducting processor is particularly noteworthy, as plasma physics simulations are notoriously computationally intensive and require high fidelity to produce meaningful results. This work could have important implications for fusion energy research and space physics applications, where understanding plasma wave dispersion is crucial for reactor design and satellite communications. The successful demonstration also validates the potential of near-term quantum devices to tackle real-world scientific computing problems when paired with sophisticated error correction methods.

An electromagnetic wave packet propagating (a) in free space, (b) from free space to a sharp jump in plasma density (which mimics the edge of a confined overdense plasma), and (c) through an inhomogeneous plasma with a spatially varying density profile. Rigetti Computing, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory and the University of Colorado, [...] The post Rigetti Simulates Plasma Wave Dispersion on Superconducting Ankaa-3 Processor Using Specialized Error Mitigation appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Machines Achieves 99.5% Two-Qubit Gate Fidelity Operating Rigetti’s Novera QPU
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Quantum Machines and Rigetti demonstrates a significant milestone in quantum hardware interoperability, showing that third-party control systems can achieve the same high-fidelity performance as native hardware configurations. The 99.5% two-qubit gate fidelity represents near-threshold performance for fault-tolerant quantum computing, where error correction schemes typically require gate fidelities above 99% to be viable. Perhaps most importantly, the ten-day automated calibration process using Quantum Machines' OPX1000 platform suggests that quantum systems are becoming more plug-and-play, potentially accelerating deployment timelines for quantum computing installations. This cross-vendor compatibility could help standardize the quantum ecosystem and give organizations more flexibility in choosing control hardware that best fits their research or commercial needs.

Quantum Machines (QM) has operated Rigetti Computing’s commercially available Novera superconducting quantum processing unit (QPU) using external control hardware and software, reaching a 99.5% median two-qubit gate fidelity. Configured via the OPX1000 orchestration platform and managed by QUAlibrate automation software, the onsite optimization process achieved full calibration within ten days. The results match the peak [...] The post Quantum Machines Achieves 99.5% Two-Qubit Gate Fidelity Operating Rigetti’s Novera QPU appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: University of Tennessee Launches Knoxville Quantum Accelerator to Anchor State Initiative
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant trend where individual states are taking bold initiatives to establish quantum technology hubs, with Tennessee's $43 million investment representing one of the more substantial state-level commitments to date. The Knoxville Quantum Accelerator's focus on hardware validation and workforce training addresses two critical gaps in the quantum ecosystem - the need for practical testing facilities and the shortage of quantum-skilled workers that many companies are facing. What makes this particularly noteworthy is how Tennessee is positioning itself to compete with traditional tech powerhouses by leveraging its advanced manufacturing base and creating localized quantum infrastructure rather than trying to attract existing coastal talent. This state-by-state approach to quantum development could reshape the geographic distribution of quantum innovation across the United States.

The University of Tennessee, Knoxville (UT) has launched the Knoxville Quantum Accelerator, branded as K-Quantum, to coordinate regional quantum technology research, hardware validation, and workforce training. The initiative operates in alignment with the Tennessee Quantum Initiative, a $43 million statewide economic strategy directed by Governor Bill Lee to establish localized infrastructure across advanced manufacturing, life [...] The post University of Tennessee Launches Knoxville Quantum Accelerator to Anchor State Initiative appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement in quantum channel discrimination: sometimes less is more
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article challenges a fundamental assumption in quantum information theory by demonstrating that entanglement isn't always beneficial for quantum channel discrimination tasks. The researchers present concrete examples where maximally entangled states perform no better than random guessing, while separable (non-entangled) states can achieve perfect discrimination between certain quantum channels. This counterintuitive finding has significant implications for quantum sensing and metrology applications, where practitioners typically assume that more entanglement leads to better performance. The work provides a systematic framework for identifying when entanglement helps versus hurts, offering practical guidance for optimizing quantum protocols in sensing and communication systems.

Entanglement is known to be a powerful resource that improves performance in various quantum information and computational tasks. A standard example of such a phenomenon is the possibility of perfectly discriminating all four Pauli operations in a single shot via the superdense coding protocol. While entanglement is often a powerful resource for quantum channel discrimination, this is not necessarily the case. In this work, we identify scenarios in which the maximally entangled state is a bad choice of input state and, more generally, show that excessive entanglement can reduce channel discriminability dramatically. To do so, we present an explicit pair of unitary channels which are perfectly discriminable without entanglement, but for which any strategy with maximally entangled input states is $ε$-close to a blind uniform guessing strategy. To develop a systematic approach, we introduce the concepts of Maximal Entanglement Worst Case (MEWC) and Maximal Entanglement Best Case (MEBC) pairs of channels, and present conditions for a pair of channels to be MEWC or MEBC. With these conditions, we show that the optimal input states for discriminating MEWC pairs of channels are necessarily separable, and provide non-trivial examples of measurement channels for which entanglement necessarily reduces the maximum probability of discrimination.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental demonstration of quantum advantage in communication complexity for Euclidean distance problem
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work represents a significant milestone in quantum communication by demonstrating the first practical quantum advantage for a real-world data analysis task - calculating distances between large datasets like images. The researchers cleverly used coherent light pulses instead of complex entangled qubits to achieve exponential communication savings for inputs as large as 100 million elements, making this one of the most scalable quantum advantage demonstrations to date. What makes this particularly exciting is that it solves an actual problem that appears frequently in machine learning and data science, showing how quantum protocols could eventually reduce bandwidth requirements for distributed computing tasks. The fact that they achieved this advantage with relatively simple optical components and real image data suggests this approach could bridge the gap between quantum theory and practical applications much sooner than previously expected.

When considering the complexity of communication protocols, the aim is to perform a certain task with the minimum amount of communication resources, such as time and transmitted information. The use of quantum states may lead to an exponential advantage in the use of such resources. Here, we are interested in the task of calculating the Euclidean distance between two vectors representing real data sets. It has been previously shown that it is possible to obtain an advantage for this task based on quantum fingerprinting. This protocol is defined in the simultaneous message passing model of communication complexity, where the two parties do not communicate with each other but send data to a third party, and exploits practical fingerprints generated using trains of coherent state pulses instead of highly entangled qubit states that are hard to generate for large input sizes needed to demonstrate an exponential advantage. We perform a proof-of-principle experimental demonstration of the Euclidean distance protocol using amplitude modulation techniques for encoding non-binary data sets and high-performance superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors required to increase the accessible input size. We show a quantum advantage in transmitted information surpassing the best classical protocol for an input size of $10^8$, for diverse types of data sets, including those corresponding to real grayscale images, and with reasonable precision and error bounds. Our results highlight the potential of quantum communication complexity for use in a broad set of applications.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: An efficient Progressive Swapping to the Middle distribution protocol adapted to imperfect quantum memories in quantum networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum networking's most fundamental challenges: how to efficiently distribute entanglement across long distances when quantum memories inevitably degrade over time. The proposed Progressive Swapping to the Middle protocol cleverly combines the best aspects of existing approaches by having entanglement swapping operations work from both ends of a network path toward the center, significantly improving success rates while maintaining reasonable fidelity levels. This work is particularly valuable because it explicitly accounts for the imperfections that plague real quantum hardware, making it more practically relevant than theoretical protocols that assume perfect conditions. As quantum networks begin transitioning from laboratory demonstrations to real-world deployments, optimization strategies like PSM will be essential for making these systems viable for applications like quantum key distribution and distributed quantum computing.

The distribution of entangled pairs of photons on the links composing a quantum network, combined with Bell state measurements and teleportation, is the basic apparatus to transfer quantum bits (qubits) over long distances. Entanglement distribution establishes an end-to-end entangled pair while consuming intermediate pairs on links and holding them for a certain time period. The technical literature identifies two main kinds of protocols, parallel and sequential ones, the latter having an advantage in resource consumption over the former. In this paper, we introduce an efficient swapping protocol called Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM) as it combines the existing Progressive Swapping (PS) protocol from both extremities of a path that meet in the middle where the received pairs are swapped. We compare PSM with two parallel protocols and PS; in our evaluation, we take into account imperfect memories and fidelity degradation. We demonstrate that PSM yields a much better link probability than PS while keeping a reasonable link fidelity, and shows an advantage in resource consumption over other protocols.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Support Vector Machine with a Scalable Quantum Kernel
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most persistent challenges in quantum machine learning: the "barren plateau" problem where quantum advantage disappears as systems scale up. The researchers' Hamming quantum kernel cleverly sidesteps the exponential concentration issue that plagues traditional fidelity-based quantum kernels by utilizing the full measurement statistics rather than relying on a single fidelity value. Their demonstration of superior performance on systems with 15-27 qubits represents a significant step toward making quantum support vector machines practically viable at meaningful scales. This work is particularly exciting because it achieves better scaling without requiring additional quantum resources, suggesting a path forward for quantum machine learning algorithms that can actually outperform classical methods on real-world problems.

Quantum support vector machines are classification algorithms that rely on quantum-generated kernels. The fidelity quantum kernel commonly used in quantum support vector machines suffers from exponential concentration as system size increases, preventing an efficient scaling beyond fewqubit systems. We introduce the Hamming quantum kernel, a classical post-processing method that is based on the same measurement outcomes as the fidelity quantum kernel. However, it avoids the exponential concentration problem by using the full measurement statistics rather than a single fidelity value. We evaluate the approach on both classical data (MNIST) and synthetic data generated from quantum circuits, using systems ranging from 2 to 27 qubits. Throughout the simulations, the Hamming quantum kernel outperforms the fidelity quantum kernel whenever 15 or more qubits are used. Furthermore, for synthetic quantum data, our method consistently outperforms the classical Gaussian kernel. This demonstrates that the Hamming quantum kernel improves the expressivity and robustness at larger qubit scales without requiring any additional quantum ressources.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Intrinsic locality dimension of quantum codes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a powerful new mathematical framework for understanding quantum error-correcting codes by defining their "intrinsic locality dimension" - a measure that captures how codes behave geometrically without being tied to specific physical layouts. The breakthrough extends fundamental theoretical limits on quantum codes beyond traditional topological codes to include promising new families like bivariate bicycle codes, which are generating excitement for their potential in near-term quantum computers. By unifying different types of codes under one mathematical umbrella, this work provides theorists and engineers with new tools to understand the fundamental trade-offs between error protection, gate operations, and hardware constraints. The research also delivers sobering news for self-correcting quantum memories, effectively ruling out certain approaches in three-dimensional systems that had been hoped to naturally resist errors without active correction.

Quantum error-correcting codes are a cornerstone of quantum computing, with broad and profound connections to physics and mathematics. In this work, we introduce the notion of intrinsic locality dimension of stabilizer codes that is independent of any background geometry and naturally incorporates flexible architectures and accommodates noninteger values, drawing on mathematical machinery from fractal geometry and geometric measure theory. Important scenarios include topological codes and algebraic codes such as bivariate-bicycle-type codes. We show how the intrinsic dimension serves as a fundamental organizing parameter that unifies code properties. In particular, we prove general limitations on code parameters and compatible fault-tolerant logical gates induced by the intrinsic dimension, generalizing the Bravyi--Poulin--Terhal and Bravyi--König bounds for regular topological codes, respectively. Furthermore, we discuss implications on thermal properties, presenting a conditional no-go result for self-correcting quantum memories in dimension $3-ε$ for any $ε>0$. Our theory lays a versatile and unifying mathematical foundation for studying the fundamental capabilities and geometric implementations of quantum error correction and fault tolerance.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UT Launches Knoxville Quantum Accelerator To Advance Tennessee’s Future
item.description:
Curator's Take: The University of Tennessee Knoxville's launch of the K-Quantum accelerator represents another significant milestone in the nationwide push to establish regional quantum hubs, following similar initiatives at universities like Yale, MIT, and the University of Chicago. This development is particularly strategic given Tennessee's proximity to Oak Ridge National Laboratory, which houses some of America's most powerful quantum computing resources and research programs. The accelerator aims to bridge the critical gap between fundamental quantum research and practical applications by fostering collaboration between academia, national labs, and industry partners. As quantum technologies mature, these regional hubs will be essential for developing the skilled workforce and innovation ecosystem needed to translate quantum advantages into real-world solutions across sectors like materials science, drug discovery, and cybersecurity.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The University of Tennessee, Knoxville, is launching the Knoxville Quantum Accelerator, also known as K-Quantum, to advance the region’s position as a leader in quantum technologies and systems. Unlike the digital computing systems we rely on today, quantum systems use elements of quantum mechanics — the complex behavior of atoms […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Fidelity bounds for spin-dependent kicks with pulsed lasers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most critical bottlenecks in trapped-ion quantum computing: achieving ultra-fast, high-fidelity two-qubit gates that could dramatically accelerate quantum computations. The authors demonstrate that spin-dependent kicks using picosecond laser pulses can achieve gate fidelities below 0.1% error rates in nanosecond timescales, which is orders of magnitude faster than traditional trapped-ion gates that typically operate on microsecond timescales. What makes this particularly significant is the identification that finite pulse duration, not ion motion, is the primary error source—a finding that provides clear engineering targets for laser system designers. These results could bring trapped-ion systems much closer to the speed requirements needed for practical fault-tolerant quantum computing, where gate times must be much shorter than qubit coherence times.

Excitation of trapped-ion hyperfine qubits with fast optical Raman pulses enables faster-than-trap-period entangling gates with qubits of long coherence time for practical quantum computation. Achieving high-fidelity fast two-qubit gates requires high-quality spin-dependent kicks (SDKs), which form their fundamental building blocks. Here, we characterize the control parameters (including Raman frequency difference, pulse arrival times, Lamb--Dicke parameter, temperature, pulse width, and SDK time) that maximize the performance of single-ion SDKs for protocols compatible with performed experiments involving a small number of fast pulses. We demonstrate through analytical methods and numerical simulations that, within the model commonly used for infidelity optimization, finite pulse duration is the dominant source of error, exceeding the contribution of secular motion by orders of magnitude for nanosecond-scale SDKs. Low infidelities -- below $10^{-3}$ for schemes with $\gtrsim10$ fixed-amplitude, equispaced, picosecond pulses -- are achievable in SDK times on the order of nanoseconds. These results provide quantitative design rules for achieving competitive SDK fidelities with current pulsed-laser technology, laying the foundation for sub-microsecond trapped-ion quantum entangling operations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Key Chemistry Question Answered, No Quantum Computer Required
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating counter-narrative in quantum computing's relationship with chemistry simulation. While quantum computers have long been touted as essential for modeling complex molecular interactions due to the quantum nature of chemical bonds, this new research demonstrates that classical algorithms can tackle problems previously thought to require quantum advantage. The finding serves as an important reminder that the race between classical and quantum approaches is far from settled, and that clever algorithmic breakthroughs on conventional computers continue to push back the boundaries of where quantum computers will truly be necessary. This development will likely reshape how researchers prioritize quantum chemistry applications and could influence funding decisions for both classical algorithm development and quantum hardware initiatives.

Do we need quantum computers to fully understand complex chemical reactions? A new result, decades in the making, shows the surprising power of ordinary “classical” machines. The post Key Chemistry Question Answered, No Quantum Computer Required first appeared on Quanta Magazine

Read original at Quanta Magazine
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qubic Announces Sale of Cryogenic Amplifiers to Quantum Machines
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Qubic and Quantum Machines highlights a critical but often overlooked aspect of quantum computing infrastructure: the specialized cryogenic amplifiers needed to read quantum states with ultra-low noise. While quantum processors grab headlines, these enabling components are essential for achieving the signal fidelity required for fault-tolerant quantum computers. The collaboration signals growing maturation in the quantum hardware supply chain, as specialized component manufacturers like Qubic emerge to support the ecosystem's scaling needs. This type of focused hardware development is crucial for moving quantum systems from research labs to practical applications where consistent, low-noise operation becomes paramount.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — – Qubic, a developer of enabling hardware for quantum computing, sensing and defence, today announces it has secured a contract with its first customer for the company’s low-noise cryogenic amplifiers. That customer is Quantum Machines, a global leader in hybrid control systems for quantum computing. Together, the companies will examine […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A charge qubit on solid neon in a spin-qubit compatible circuit QED platform
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant step toward realizing spin qubits on solid neon, a platform that could potentially offer exceptionally long coherence times by isolating electrons from the material defects that plague conventional semiconductor qubits. The researchers achieved an impressive order-of-magnitude improvement in Rabi frequencies compared to previous work, demonstrating much stronger coupling between the electron and microwave control fields - a crucial requirement for fast, high-fidelity quantum operations. While challenges remain in precisely positioning electrons on the neon surface, the successful integration with a magnetic-field-compatible superconducting resonator brings this exotic qubit platform closer to practical quantum computing applications. The work highlights how exploring unconventional qubit platforms beyond silicon and superconducting circuits could unlock new pathways to more coherent quantum processors.

Electrons floating in vacuum provide a clean platform for quantum information processing due to their isolation from material defects; electrons on solid neon have emerged as a promising qubit platform for its long coherence times. Here, toward spin-qubit realization, we couple a single electron on solid neon to a magnetic-field-compatible superconducting NbTiN nanowire resonator. We realize a charge qubit and demonstrate microwave readout and coherent control, with Rabi frequencies up to 76 MHz, an order of magnitude larger than in previous studies. Under strong driving, we observe a qubit frequency shift from nonlinear interactions with the intense microwave field. Deterministic electron trapping at an intended position remains challenging due to solid neon surface roughness; we characterize the electron's position from its differential coupling to distinct electrodes. Although not trapped at an intended position, our estimates indicate that spin-qubit demonstrations remain feasible.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Massachusetts Invests $25 Million in MIT Quantum Lab
item.description:
Curator's Take: This $25 million investment represents Massachusetts doubling down on its quantum leadership at a crucial inflection point for the field. With quantum computing rapidly transitioning from research labs to commercial applications, state-level investments like this help ensure regions can compete for the talent and infrastructure needed to build a quantum economy. MIT's new Quantum Systems Laboratory will likely focus on bridging the gap between fundamental quantum research and practical systems engineering, addressing one of the field's most pressing challenges. This move positions Massachusetts to compete directly with other quantum hubs like IBM's New York corridor and Google's California operations as the race for quantum advantage intensifies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — MIT President Sally Kornbluth and Massachusetts Governor Maura Healey announced plans for a new laboratory to accelerate the development of next-generation quantum technologies that will enable Massachusetts to remain a national hub for quantum innovation. Speaking at the Samberg Conference Center on campus, the leaders introduced the Quantum Systems Laboratory […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum State Preparation via Neural Network Encoding in Quantum Machine Learning
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum machine learning's most fundamental bottlenecks - the prohibitively expensive process of loading classical data into quantum states, which typically requires optimizing a new quantum circuit for every single data point. The researchers' clever solution trains a classical neural network once to directly predict the quantum circuit parameters needed for any input, eliminating the need for iterative optimization during inference and achieving a remarkable 5000-fold speedup. This breakthrough could finally make quantum machine learning practical for real applications, as the method maintains high fidelity while transforming state preparation from a per-instance optimization problem into a simple forward pass through a pre-trained network. The validation on standard image datasets like MNIST demonstrates the approach works on realistic data, potentially unlocking quantum advantages for machine learning tasks that were previously computationally infeasible due to the state preparation overhead.

A central challenge in quantum machine learning is the state preparation bottleneck that describes the prohibitive computational cost of loading high-dimensional classical data into a quantum state. Although amplitude encoding can represent $2^n$-dimensional data using only $n$ qubits in principle, preparing arbitrary states remains computationally expensive, typically requiring variational optimization of a parameterized quantum circuit for each individual data instance. In this work, we propose a method that avoids iterative optimization by training a classical neural network to map input data directly to the continuous parameters of a fixed quantum circuit. We demonstrate the generation of quantum image states with high fidelity on data not seen during training. Since all optimization is performed once during training, the resulting model encodes new inputs in a single inference step, providing a scalable pathway for data loading in near-term quantum algorithms. We validate our method on the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets, achieving fidelities up to 0.992 on unseen images and reducing the per-data-instance runtime by more than 5000-fold.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Foxconn Expects Quantum Commercialization in 2030
item.description:
Curator's Take: This timeline from Foxconn offers a fascinating window into how major electronics manufacturers view quantum computing's commercial trajectory, with the Taiwanese giant betting on 2030 as the inflection point for widespread adoption. Given Foxconn's unparalleled experience in scaling complex technologies for global markets through their manufacturing partnerships with Apple and other tech leaders, their assessment carries significant weight in understanding when quantum systems might transition from lab curiosities to production-ready solutions. The fact that Foxconn's Hon Hai Research Institute is already producing internationally recognized quantum research suggests they're positioning themselves as more than just a future manufacturer, but as a key player in the quantum supply chain ecosystem that will be crucial for bringing quantum computers to market at scale.

Insider Brief Foxconn’s quantum computing ambitions are drawing attention beyond Taiwan, but company executives say the path from research success to commercial business remains a multi-year effort. According to DigiTimes Asia, Foxconn Chairman Young Liu said the company’s work through the Hon Hai Research Institute has produced research that has been adopted internationally and cited […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quandela Adds Industry Veterans to Leadership And Governance Teams
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals Quandela's transition from a promising quantum startup to a company positioning for commercial scale-up, with leadership appointments that bring deep operational and industrialization expertise from major tech companies like OVHcloud. The addition of veterans focused on manufacturing and operations suggests Quandela is moving beyond research milestones toward building production-ready photonic quantum systems, which is particularly significant given photonics' potential advantages in room-temperature operation and networking applications. These strategic hires reflect the broader maturation of the quantum computing industry, where companies are now prioritizing commercial execution alongside technological innovation. The emphasis on industrialization expertise specifically indicates Quandela may be preparing for larger-scale deployment of their photonic quantum computers in the coming years.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quandela, a European leader in photonic quantum computing, today announced a major reinforcement of its leadership and governance with the appointment of Cyril Dujardin as Chief Operating Officer and Michel Zecri as Vice President of Industrialization. Michel Paulin, former CEO of OVHcloud, SFR and Neuf Cegetel, becomes Chairman of the Board. These appointments mark a […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Top Quantum Computing Jobs and Salaries in 2026
item.description:
Curator's Take: This workforce analysis reveals quantum computing's transition from academic curiosity to commercial reality, with nearly 16,500 professionals now working in pure-play quantum companies worldwide. The growth in defined job roles and competitive salaries signals that the industry has reached a critical inflection point where practical applications are driving genuine employment demand rather than speculative investment. For aspiring quantum professionals, this data provides valuable insight into which skills are most marketable and where the field is heading, while established tech workers can gauge when and how to pivot into this rapidly expanding sector. The formalization of quantum career paths also suggests the technology is maturing beyond research labs into scalable business applications.

Insider Brief Quantum computing is no longer a field that exists only in research papers. Companies are hiring, salaries are rising, and the roles themselves are becoming more defined. According to QED-C’s State of the Global Quantum Industry 2026 report, the global pure-play quantum workforce reached nearly 16,500 professionals in 2025 – an increase of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Update on Quantinuum’s IPO Filing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article provides crucial insight into Quantinuum's public offering process, marking a potential watershed moment for the quantum computing industry as one of its leading hardware companies moves toward becoming publicly traded. The updated S-1 filing likely contains revised financial projections and operational details that will give investors and the broader quantum community a clearer picture of the commercial viability of trapped-ion quantum computing systems. Quantinuum's IPO could serve as a bellwether for how public markets value quantum computing companies, potentially influencing funding and valuation expectations across the entire sector. The financial transparency required in these filings also offers rare visibility into the business metrics of a major quantum hardware player, providing valuable benchmarks for the industry's maturation.

On May 11, 2026 we reported on the first public S-1 filing that Quantinuum submitted to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) that provided much detail on the company's technology and operations. Previously, the company announced it had submitted a confidential S-1 in April. In the first public S-1 filing, much of the financial [...] The post Update on Quantinuum’s IPO Filing appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Real-Time Quantum Error Correction System Stack: Architecture, Algorithms, and Engineering Practice
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive white paper tackles quantum computing's most pressing engineering challenge: building real-time quantum error correction systems that can actually scale beyond laboratory demonstrations. While recent breakthroughs from Google, Riverlane, and Rigetti have proven that quantum error correction works in principle, this research reveals that the real bottlenecks lie not in decoder algorithms themselves but in system-level engineering challenges like end-to-end latency coordination and tail performance optimization. The proposed six-layer reference architecture provides the quantum computing field with its first systematic framework for building production-ready error correction systems, essentially serving as a blueprint for the transition from experimental quantum computers to fault-tolerant machines that can run meaningful algorithms. This work represents a crucial shift from proving quantum error correction is possible to actually engineering systems that can deliver it reliably at scale.

Quantum error correction (QEC) is transitioning from physical feasibility demonstrations to systems engineering challenges. Google has achieved below-threshold performance on distance-5/7 surface codes, while Riverlane and Rigetti have demonstrated hardware-integrated low-latency feedback loops. These milestones indicate that the core challenge of real-time decoding has shifted from algorithmic capability to system-level engineering. However, a substantial engineering gap remains between laboratory demonstrations and scalable fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC). This white paper addresses three questions: (1) Where are the real bottlenecks in real-time QEC: beyond average decoder speed, the constraints lie in QEC round time, tail latency, and end-to-end data path coordination; (2) How mature are mainstream decoder algorithms: we benchmark the major decoders for both surface codes and quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes, evaluating their real-time readiness; (3) What system stack do we propose: a six-layer reference architecture from syndrome acquisition to logical operations, with interface definitions and latency budget models. Our results quantify the gap between current decoder performance and real-time requirements, and identify the architectural choices needed to close it.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How To Track Qubits Through Space and Time (Or: Sailing in a Quantum Boat)
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles a fascinating loophole in quantum cryptography: existing position verification protocols can't actually prove a quantum device is physically at a specific location, since distributed attackers could potentially fool the system by working together across multiple locations. The researchers introduce "quantum localization" protocols that ensure a unique, unclonable quantum state exists at a verified spacetime point and nowhere else, opening the door to tracking quantum information as it moves through space and time like following a quantum breadcrumb trail. Beyond the obvious applications in secure communications and quantum key distribution, the concept of "functionality localization" could enable revolutionary scenarios where sensitive computations can only be performed at specific physical locations, potentially transforming how we think about secure distributed computing in a quantum world.

While quantum position verification aims to certify a prover's location using quantum information, existing security definitions only guarantee that part of the successful adversarial party is in the claimed location. This leaves open the possibility that a distributed team of adversaries can jointly simulate a prover in a way that defeats the intended meaning of ``being at a location'' in position-based cryptography. We introduce stronger notions of position verification that we call quantum localization, which requires that there is a specified, unclonable state at the verified spacetime point -- and that this state can be found nowhere else. We show that quantum localization leads naturally to a meaningful notion of trajectory verification, in which quantum information is verifiably tracked through space and time. We construct quantum localization and trajectory verification protocols using quantum anchor states, which generalize coset states from unclonable cryptography. The security of our schemes is proven in the classical oracle (i.e. ideal obfuscation) model, which can be heuristically instantiated in the plain model using post-quantum indistinguishability obfuscation. We also introduce and instantiate the concept of functionality localization, which guarantees that the adversary has the ability to compute a secret function at the verified spacetime point, and this function cannot be computed anywhere else. This raises the intriguing possibility of localizing computational capabilities in space and time. More broadly, we believe our notions of quantum localization and our feasibility results provide stronger foundations for position-based cryptography.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Research progress on quantum neural networks and quantum machine learning
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive survey arrives at a crucial moment when quantum machine learning is transitioning from theoretical promise to practical experimentation, offering researchers a much-needed roadmap through the rapidly expanding landscape of quantum neural network architectures. The paper's systematic comparison of performance metrics across different QNN types - from quantum convolutional networks to quantum Boltzmann machines - provides valuable guidance for practitioners trying to navigate which quantum approaches might actually deliver advantages for specific machine learning tasks. What makes this particularly timely is that major quantum computing companies are actively developing quantum machine learning platforms, making the practical insights about training time and resource requirements increasingly relevant for near-term quantum applications. The survey effectively captures how quantum neural networks are evolving beyond simple proof-of-concept demonstrations toward architectures that could potentially handle real-world data analysis challenges.

Machine learning holds fundamental computational significance due to the increasing demand for efficient solutions to complex tasks in data analysis, pattern recognition, and optimization, which are essential for addressing the multifaceted challenges of modern society. As the volume of data proliferates at an unprecedented rate, the need for more powerful machine learning strategies becomes increasingly evident. Quantum neural networks (QNNs) represent an emerging and transformative research field that seeks to harness the unique principles of quantum mechanics to enhance the capabilities of machine learning algorithms. This survey examines various QNN approaches, including fully connected QNNs, quantum convolutional neural networks, equivariant QNNs, quantum Hopfield networks, quantum Boltzmann machines, quantum reservoir computing, and composite networks for quantum reinforcement learning, quantum generative learning, and quantum transfer learning. We summarize the relevant investigations on their performance, including learning accuracy, training time, and resource requirements, etc. Each QNN type has unique strengths and weaknesses, offering diverse solutions for different applications.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Multiparameter quantum estimation and entanglement in top--antitop quark production
item.description:
Curator's Take: This fascinating study reveals how particle physics experiments at the Large Hadron Collider could serve as unexpected quantum sensing platforms, using entangled top-antitop quark pairs to simultaneously measure multiple relativistic parameters with quantum-enhanced precision. The work demonstrates that the spin correlations naturally produced in high-energy particle collisions create genuine quantum entanglement that can be leveraged for multiparameter estimation tasks, potentially opening entirely new applications for quantum metrology beyond traditional quantum computing hardware. What makes this particularly intriguing is the proposal to extract quantum information from particle collision data that's already being collected at CERN, suggesting we might be sitting on a goldmine of quantum sensing data without realizing it. This bridges fundamental physics research with quantum information science in a way that could inform both our understanding of quantum correlations in nature and the development of new quantum sensing protocols.

We investigate the interplay between quantum correlations and multiparameter quantum estimation in top--antitop quark pair production through the gluon-fusion channel. Using the spin density matrix formalism, we construct an effective two-qubit quantum state governed by the relativistic parameters associated with the scattering process. Within the framework of quantum metrology, we derive the quantum Fisher information matrix for the simultaneous estimation of the relativistic velocity parameter and the production angle, and we analyze the corresponding quantum precision bounds. Our results reveal highly nontrivial estimation regimes strongly controlled by relativistic spin correlations and scattering geometry. We further characterize the produced state through the concurrence and demonstrate the existence of strong connections between entanglement structures and multiparameter estimation sensitivity. Finally, we discuss the experimental feasibility of probing these effects at the Large Hadron Collider through spin-correlation observables and reconstructed top--antitop density matrices. Our results identify top--antitop production as a unique relativistic platform for exploring quantum information theory and multiparameter quantum metrology in high-energy physics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental Implementation of the Quantum Volunteer's Dilemma on NISQ Hardware: Noise Analysis and Digital-Twin Validation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article demonstrates a significant milestone for quantum game theory by successfully implementing a complex multiplayer quantum game on real NISQ hardware with up to 9 players, showing that quantum advantages in strategic scenarios can survive the harsh realities of current quantum computers. The researchers achieved remarkable resilience to noise, maintaining quantum advantages over classical strategies even as fidelity degraded, which suggests that certain quantum algorithms may be more robust to errors than previously thought. Particularly intriguing is their finding that single-qubit errors dominate at smaller scales while multi-qubit correlations become problematic only at larger system sizes, providing valuable insights for designing noise-resilient quantum protocols. The work also exposes critical limitations in current quantum simulation methods, as their digital twin model failed to capture the true hardware behavior at scale, highlighting the ongoing need for better noise characterization tools in the NISQ era.

We present an experimental implementation of the multiplayer Quantum Volunteer's Dilemma on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) hardware, executed on the ibm_kingston backend via Qiskit Runtime. The game is evaluated for N = 2 to 9 players under four transpiler optimization levels, with 20 independent repetitions per configuration and 2048 shots per circuit, including post-processing readout error correction via mthree. Target-state fidelity decays with system size but remains above 70% (corrected) through N = 9. With readout correction, the global average payoff reproduces the quantum theoretical benchmark exactly for N <= 6 and exceeds the classical Nash equilibrium across the full tested range. Optimization level 2 is selected as the reference configuration after gate count analysis reveals that levels 2 and 3 produce identical transpiled circuits, with level 2 achieving superior fidelity stability. A Hamming distance analysis of raw measurement counts shows that single-qubit errors dominate at small N, with multi-qubit contributions growing beyond N = 6. A calibration-based digital twin captures global payoff trends but exhibits a linear fidelity decay profile that diverges from the hardware behavior at large N, exposing the limits of first-order independent per-qubit noise models. These results demonstrate that aggregate quantum advantage in multiplayer games is robust to NISQ noise conditions across the full tested range, while the practical observability of state-level advantage is constrained to N <= 8 under post-processed readout correction.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Beyond a Single Quantum Chip: Why the Future of Quantum Computing is Modular
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical shift happening in quantum computing hardware development - the move away from cramming ever more qubits onto single chips toward modular, interconnected quantum systems. As we approach the limits of what's physically practical on individual quantum processors, companies like IBM, IonQ, and others are exploring how to network multiple quantum units together to achieve true scalability. This modular approach could be the key to reaching the hundreds of thousands or millions of qubits needed for fault-tolerant quantum computers, making it one of the most important architectural discussions in the field today. The perspective from Firgun Ventures offers valuable insight into why investors and technologists are betting on distributed quantum systems rather than monolithic super-chips.

Guest Post by Zeynep Koruturk, Dr. Kris Naudts, and Donald Harmitt of Firgun Ventures For years, the headline metric in quantum computing has been a simple one: how many qubits can a company fit onto a single chip. Qubits are the basic units of quantum information, and increasing their number signals that the field is [...] The post Beyond a Single Quantum Chip: Why the Future of Quantum Computing is Modular appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Educators Named to Chattanooga/Hamilton County’s Inaugural QCaMP Cohort
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement represents a crucial step in addressing one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: building a pipeline of quantum-literate students and workers for the future. By training K-12 educators in quantum concepts through Sandia National Laboratories' QCaMP program, Chattanooga is positioning itself as a regional quantum education hub while tackling the fundamental issue that quantum technologies are advancing faster than our educational systems can adapt. The collaboration between the Department of Energy's Quantum Systems Accelerator and local educators demonstrates how federal quantum initiatives are beginning to reach into communities, potentially creating a more geographically distributed quantum workforce. This grassroots approach to quantum literacy could serve as a model for other regions looking to participate in the emerging quantum economy rather than leaving quantum education concentrated only in traditional tech hubs.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The Chattanooga Quantum Collaborative (CQC) announced today the first Chattanooga/Hamilton County-area cohort of educators participating in QCaMP (Quantum Computing, Mathematics and Physics Camp), a professional learning experience designed by Sandia National Laboratories as part of the U.S. Department of Energy’s Quantum Systems Accelerator (QSA), to help K–12 teachers explore emerging […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qilimanjaro Deploys an Analog Quantum Computer at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center
item.description:
Curator's Take: This deployment marks a significant milestone in the diversification of quantum computing approaches, as analog quantum computers offer distinct advantages for solving optimization problems and simulating complex physical systems compared to their digital counterparts. The integration at Barcelona Supercomputing Center's MareNostrum-Ona facility creates a unique hybrid environment where researchers can directly compare and potentially combine analog and digital quantum approaches on the same platform. Qilimanjaro's analog system joins the growing ecosystem of European quantum infrastructure, providing researchers with access to a technology particularly well-suited for near-term applications in materials science, drug discovery, and logistics optimization. The placement in the historic Torre Girona chapel also symbolizes the blend of cutting-edge technology with Europe's commitment to making quantum computing accessible through major research institutions.

MareNostrum-Ona, the quantum computing partition of MareNostrum 5, housed in the Torre Girona chapel at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center. The red and blue systems are the digital quantum computers; the green system in the centre is the newly inaugurated analog quantum computer.Credit: BSC-CNS Qilimanjaro Quantum Tech has officially inaugurated a new analog quantum computer at [...] The post Qilimanjaro Deploys an Analog Quantum Computer at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Metasurfaces for neutral-atom trapping
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a potential game-changer for scaling neutral-atom quantum computers, which currently face significant engineering bottlenecks in creating the massive arrays needed for practical quantum applications. Optical metasurfaces offer a compelling solution by providing vastly superior pixel counts compared to traditional spatial light modulators, enabling researchers to create hundreds of thousands of optical traps for individual atoms rather than the hundreds typically achieved today. The ability to precisely control light at this scale could finally unlock the path toward utility-scale neutral-atom quantum processors, while also enabling more compact and integrated quantum systems. This represents a crucial intersection of photonics and quantum computing that could accelerate the timeline for building fault-tolerant quantum computers with the atom counts needed for real-world applications.

Trapped neutral atoms are one of the leading platforms for quantum information technologies, in particular for quantum computing, but scaling them to array sizes needed for utility-scale quantum computing is a major engineering challenge. Here we review optical metasurfaces as an enabling technology that provides fine control over the phase, amplitude, and polarization of light, with pixel counts far exceeding what is available with spatial light modulators (SLMs) and other active devices. The large pixel counts have recently led to demonstrations of arrays of optical tweezers with hundreds of thousands of sites and arrays of optical bottle-beams with complex three-dimensional trapping profiles. The flexibility and scalability of optical metasurfaces provides a route towards miniaturized, integrated, and highly scalable atomic experiments and instruments.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A Denser Planar Surface Code
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a major breakthrough in quantum error correction efficiency, demonstrating how clever geometric arrangements of surface code defects on a hexagonal lattice can pack 4.5 times more logical qubits into the same physical space compared to standard approaches. The work bridges theory and practice by showing these dense codes can enable practical quantum chemistry simulations, specifically calculating the electronic structure of the FeMoco nitrogen-fixation catalyst using 89,000 superconducting qubits in under a month. What makes this particularly exciting is the 36-fold reduction in physical qubit requirements compared to previous estimates, bringing utility-scale quantum chemistry applications significantly closer to reality with near-term hardware capabilities. The researchers have essentially found a way to get much more computational power from the same quantum hardware footprint, which could accelerate the timeline for achieving quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We present a quantum code implementable on a regular $2$D hex grid with an estimated encoding rate up to $4.5\times$ of that of a rotated surface code patch using circuit-level noise in a one- and two-qubit $10^{-3}$ error uniform depolarizing model. Our approach is based on yoking a dense packing of surface code twist defects, enabled by new stabilizer measurement cycles with an optimal four layers of nearest-neighbor two-qubit gates, almost no distance-reducing hook errors, and efficient decoding. We demonstrate a space-efficient architecture for computing on densely packed logical qubits, including new padding-free lattice surgery protocols in an optimal bounding box of $2d^2$ data and measurement qubits per patch. Assuming a $1μ$s surface code cycle time and a $10μ$s reaction time, these developments enable chemically accurate ground state phase estimation of a broad class of `utility-scale' electronic structure simulation problems such as the $108$ spin-orbital FeMoco-based nitrogen fixation catalyst in under a month with $89$k noisy superconducting qubits. We elucidate a Pareto frontier of space-time trade-offs and find a minimum physical quantum volume of $1.3$ mega-qubit-hours. These correspond to a $36\times$ space and $6.6\times$ spacetime improvement, respectively, over our previous state-of-the-art minimum-Toffoli resource estimates (Phys. Rev. X 15, 041016).

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qubit-efficient variational algorithm for nuclear structure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant step toward using quantum computers to solve one of physics' most computationally demanding problems: predicting the structure of atomic nuclei. The researchers successfully demonstrated variational quantum algorithms running on real IBM quantum hardware with up to 26 qubits to calculate ground state energies of boron-10 and carbon-12 nuclei, achieving impressive accuracy of 0.21% error for the best mapping strategy. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the systematic comparison of three different qubit-mapping approaches, revealing that the choice of how to translate nuclear physics problems onto quantum circuits dramatically affects both resource requirements and accuracy on near-term noisy devices. This work bridges quantum computing and nuclear physics in a practical way, potentially opening paths toward quantum simulations of heavier nuclei that are currently impossible to model classically due to the exponential growth of computational complexity.

In this work, we compare three qubit-mapping strategies to study the structure of the nuclear ground state within the shell model description employing the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) approach. Although the initial point for different mappings is a Hamiltonian matrix in many-body particle basis or Slater determinant (SD) basis, the structure of the trial wavefunction and resource counts are different for each mapping. These three mappings are tested for a mid $p$-shell nucleus $^{10}$B and compared the quantum resources required to find the ground state for each mapping. Further, we extend the qubit-efficient mapping to study the ground state of one more mid $p$-shell nucleus $^{12}$C. We run circuits up to 26-qubits representing their ground states on a noisy simulator (IBM's FakeFez backend) and quantum hardware ($ibm\_fez$). The best post-error mitigated results from the hardware for $^{10}$B ground state is obtained following SD to qubit mapping with a percent error of 0.21 \%. The percent errors for the same state following cSD and pnSD mapping are 3.37 and 8.88 \%, respectively. On the other hand, following the cSD mapping, the post-error mitigated ground state energy of $^{12}$C is 6.82 \% away from the exact result. We further evaluate the fidelity of the VQE wavefunctions obtained from hardware with respect to the shell model wavefunctions for the cSD mapping. This cSD mapping can be useful for scaling the VQE algorithm for complex nuclei across different mass regions in terms of qubit efficiency.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qilimanjaro Inaugurates an Analog Quantum Computer at Barcelona Supercomputing Center
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a significant milestone as Qilimanjaro adds analog quantum capabilities to complement their existing digital quantum system at Barcelona Supercomputing Center, creating one of the most comprehensive quantum computing environments in Europe. Analog quantum computers excel at solving optimization problems and simulating physical systems by using quantum states that continuously vary rather than discrete quantum bits, making them particularly valuable for materials science and complex scheduling challenges. The integration with Europe's powerful MareNostrum 5 supercomputer positions Barcelona as a major quantum research hub and demonstrates how hybrid classical-quantum-analog computing architectures may become the norm for tackling different types of computational problems. This deployment reflects the growing recognition that the future of quantum computing likely involves multiple quantum paradigms working together rather than a single dominant technology.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Today, the new analog quantum computer built by Qilimanjaro Quantum Tech was inaugurated at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC-CNS), home to MareNostrum 5, one of the most powerful supercomputers in Europe. The deployment expands BSC’s quantum infrastructure, complementing the digital quantum system previously installed by Qilimanjaro and GMV in 2025 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Non-Abelian Mixer for QAOA on Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fascinating frontier in quantum computing by developing specialized algorithms for hybrid systems that combine both continuous-variable oscillators and discrete qubits, representing a significant step beyond traditional all-qubit approaches. The team's non-Abelian mixer for QAOA demonstrates consistent improvements in solving optimization problems like Max-Cut, suggesting that hybrid platforms could offer genuine algorithmic advantages rather than just being alternative hardware implementations. This work is particularly noteworthy because hybrid oscillator-qubit systems are emerging as promising candidates for practical quantum computing, with companies like Xanadu exploring similar architectures, making algorithm development for these platforms increasingly crucial. The consistent performance gains across different graph sizes indicate this approach could scale effectively, potentially opening new pathways for quantum optimization algorithms that leverage the unique properties of mixed discrete-continuous quantum systems.

The realization of universal control in hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors enables the systematic design and implementation of quantum algorithms. However, the algorithmic development for such platforms remains at an early stage. While the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) has been extensively studied in both continuous-variable (CV) and discrete-variable (DV) quantum systems, its development in the hybrid CV-DV setting remains limited. In this paper, we propose a hardware-native non-Abelian mixer for QAOA on hybrid CV-DV quantum processors and develop a corresponding hybrid ansatz for the Max-Cut problem. We evaluate the proposed ansatz on unweighted Erdős-Rényi graphs and benchmark it against the standard transverse-field mixer using the approximation ratio and optimal-solution probability. Across all graph sizes and Fock cutoffs in our simulations, the proposed non-Abelian mixer consistently improves both expected solution quality and the probability of sampling an optimal solution relative to the transverse-field mixer. These results indicate that the proposed non-Abelian mixer is a promising building block for QAOA on hybrid oscillator-qubit platforms.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Heralded ultrafast generation of macroscopic quantum states in matter with bright squeezed vacuum light
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates a clever workaround to one of quantum optics' persistent challenges: how to quickly prepare large-scale quantum states in matter without losing coherence to unwanted entanglement with light. By using bright squeezed vacuum light combined with strategic measurements, the team shows they can herald the creation of macroscopic quantum superposition states in matter much faster than conventional methods, potentially reaching timescales relevant for practical quantum sensing applications. The ability to generate cat-like quantum states and Dicke states on demand could significantly advance quantum metrology, where such exotic matter states promise sensitivity improvements beyond what classical sensors can achieve. This work bridges fundamental quantum optics with practical quantum technology, offering a new toolkit for engineering quantum matter states that could enhance everything from gravitational wave detection to medical imaging.

We show that bright squeezed vacuum light, combined with a single-shot quadrature measurement of the post-interaction light, enables the ultrafast generation of macroscopic quantum states in matter. Although in the weak-coupling regime multiphoton quantum light leaves the unconditional matter state as a classical mixture due to light--matter entanglement, quadrature-based heralding prepares the matter in a Gaussian-weighted quantum superposition. For an ensemble of resonantly electric-dipole-coupled two-level systems, this heralding dynamics acts as a Gaussian filter with respect to the electric polarization, with brighter squeezed-vacuum light accelerating the preparation of the zero-eigenvalue Dicke state. Counter-rotating terms further drive a stroboscopic transition from this Dicke state to a cat-like state. Our results open a route to ultrafast engineering of macroscopic quantum matter with strong-field quantum light.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Programmable Dissipation via Partial Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research presents a clever paradigm shift that turns quantum computing's greatest challenge - noise and decoherence - into a controllable tool for simulating open quantum systems. Rather than fighting to eliminate all errors through traditional fault-tolerant quantum computing, the authors demonstrate how to intentionally program specific types of "logical noise" by manipulating the error correction process itself, essentially using the quantum computer's natural tendency toward decoherence as a feature rather than a bug. This approach could dramatically reduce the resource requirements for simulating realistic quantum systems that naturally interact with their environment, such as biological molecules or materials under real-world conditions. The work bridges two previously opposing goals in quantum computing - achieving fault tolerance and accurately modeling dissipative quantum dynamics - opening new possibilities for near-term quantum advantage in quantum simulation applications.

Noise is typically treated as the adversary of quantum information processing. For open quantum dynamics, however, dissipation is part of the target physics, creating a tension with fault-tolerant architectures designed to suppress decoherence. Here we show that logical noise can instead be turned into a calibrated resource. We treat the error-correction cycle as a programmable primitive: one fault-tolerant round induces a logical completely positive trace-preserving map, and decoder/recovery randomization generates a controllable family of logical channels whose convex mixtures realize Kraus-channel mixing. This enables direct compilation of target dissipators into effective logical dynamics without explicit ancilla qubits for encoding the bath degree of freedoms. We derive an accuracy criterion for multi-step simulation in which the code distance is chosen so that uncontrolled logical errors remain a small fraction of the intended dissipation per step, rather than being driven below an arbitrarily small closed-system tolerance. Partial quantum error correction thus repurposes fault-tolerant structure to sculpt dissipation, offering a resource-efficient route to quantum simulation of open quantum systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Overcoming the Matrix-Product-State Encoding Barrier via DMRG-Guided Probabilistic Imaginary-Time Evolution
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum simulation's most persistent challenges: efficiently preparing ground states on quantum computers without drowning in circuit complexity. The researchers cleverly combine classical DMRG preprocessing with quantum probabilistic evolution, essentially using the best of both worlds to avoid the exponential scaling nightmare that typically plagues matrix product state encodings on quantum hardware. What makes this particularly compelling is their discovery of an optimal "stopping point" for the encoding process, beyond which additional circuit layers yield diminishing returns—a practical insight that could significantly reduce the resource requirements for near-term quantum simulations. The framework represents a promising step toward making quantum advantage achievable for real condensed matter problems, where ground state preparation is often the computational bottleneck.

Ground-state preparation is a fundamental task in quantum simulation, because the overlap of the prepared state with the true ground state significantly affects the overall cost of subsequent quantum algorithms. We propose a three-stage framework in which a matrix product state (MPS) of an $N$-site system obtained by the density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) is loaded onto an $N$-qubit quantum register through an optimization-free matrix product disentangler (MPD) encoding circuit, and the residual error is then reduced by probabilistic imaginary-time evolution (PITE). We demonstrate that the central-bond Schmidt rank of intermediate states during MPS encoding grows logistically with the number of layers. Its inflection point $L^{*}$ marks the boundary of the efficient encoding regime. Beyond this point, the gain in fidelity slows rapidly, and the number of additional MPD layers required to reach a target infidelity $\varepsilon$ empirically scales as $\mathcal{O}(N^5\log(N/\varepsilon))$. To avoid this encoding-only tail, we stop the encoder at $L^{*}$ and suppress the remaining excited-state components by PITE, with the linear PITE schedule fixed deterministically from the ground-state energy, the effective gap, and the reference overlap estimated by DMRG. Numerical experiments on the spin-$1/2$ staggered-field Heisenberg chain show that the framework avoids very deep encoding circuits and substantially suppresses the post-selection overhead intrinsic to PITE. Combining classical preprocessing by DMRG, optimization-free MPS encoding, and deterministically scheduled PITE, the present framework offers a practical hybrid route to ground-state preparation in quantum simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly
